topic: A person learns their college is closing down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The final board meeting felt like a personal assault. I watched Professor Albright, usually so composed, nervously smooth the edges of the termination notice. My stomach twisted into a tight, cold knot. All those late-night study sessions, the shared triumphs in the library, the sense of belonging—it was all dissolving into this sterile, administrative formality. I kept glancing at Maya, who was laughing easily with another student, her posture radiating an effortless connection I desperately craved. A sharp, involuntary clench tightened my jaw. "This can't be happening," I muttered under my breath, the sound barely audible over the shuffling papers. I forced a bright, brittle smile, but my hands were shaking so badly I had to grip the edge of the table to steady them.

[story 2]

The campus news feed was a relentless parade of closure announcements. Every headline was a hammer blow against my fragile sense of security. I paced my dorm room, the worn carpet feeling suddenly suffocating beneath my bare feet. I picked up my phone, intending to scroll through social media, but instead, I found myself replaying conversations—conversations where others seemed to effortlessly navigate the academic landscape, where their successes felt so natural. A hot flush crept up my neck. I slammed the phone onto the desk, the sound echoing in the quiet room. "Why them?" I whispered to the empty air, the accusation heavy in my tone. It felt like a fundamental injustice, a wrong turn in the universe that had singled me out for this sudden ending.

[story 3]

I stood by the main quad, watching the last few students gather their belongings, their faces a mixture of resignation and quiet relief. A strange, bitter taste coated my tongue. I watched Liam, who was hugging a friend goodbye, his shoulders broad and relaxed. He seemed so unburdened by this impending collapse. I felt a sudden, intense urge to disrupt the scene, to inject some chaos, to make the reality of the situation feel less final. My knuckles were white where I gripped my backpack straps. "It’s not fair," I thought, the sentiment sharp and undeniable. I took a deep, shaky breath, trying to force my chest to expand, but it felt constricted, as if something vital had been squeezed out of me.
